summary: In times of economic uncertainty and business volatility, data teams must shift from peacetime exploration to wartime survival mode, focusing on singular business-critical missions. Drawing from personal experiences during the 2008 financial crisis and COVID, Reis argues that data professionals should deliver quick visible wins, stay scrappy, and be integral to business survival rather than hiding in technical siloes.
key_points: Data teams should have singular focus on company survival missions during economic uncertainty
Quick, visible wins are essential - don't hide in the shadows with technical work
Be scrappy and resourceful with existing tools rather than requesting new technology
Collapse organizational boundaries and work directly with product, engineering, and leadership
These wartime principles should actually be practiced in peacetime too - stay lean and mean
money_quotes: In peacetime, leaders must maximize and broaden the current opportunity... In wartime, by contrast, the company typically has a single bullet in the chamber and must, at all costs, hit the target.
Things in business can turn on a dime. Black swans are more common than you think. Once in a generation crises happen every few years.
The big lesson: don't take things for granted and avoid complacency. Understand how you can succeed, and be paranoid about how it can all come crashing down.
You have the advantage of knowing the data better than anyone. Find ways to leverage your domain expertise to help the business. Be an integral part of the business.
Most business books are fluff, written by people with more luck than smarts.
key_insight: Data teams must abandon peacetime luxuries of exploration and tool accumulation, instead becoming scrappy, business-focused operators who deliver quick wins that directly support company survival.
